assignment.name: ml_notebook
assignment.title: Train and evaluate a small image classifier on CIFAR-10
assignment.description: Produce a Jupyter notebook that loads CIFAR-10, trains an image classifier from scratch, and reports test accuracy with a confusion matrix and at least one ablation comparing two design choices.
constraints: Single notebook, runnable end-to-end on a free Colab T4 in under 30 minutes.
No pretrained weights; the model must be trained from scratch.
Final reported test accuracy must exceed 70%.
Include one ablation study (e.g. with vs. without data augmentation).
All hyperparameters declared in a single config cell at the top.

verdict.notes: ResNet-18 from scratch on CIFAR-10 is conceptually sound and will exceed 70% accuracy, but the plan is frontloaded with a high-capacity model that may obscure learning about design choices. The ablation (augmentation vs. none) is clear and valuable, but a second ablation comparing architectural or regularization variants would strengthen the conceptual depth. The 5-day timeline is comfortable for this scope—verify that 30-min Colab runtime is realistic; ResNet-18 for 200 epochs on T4 is tight and may require learning rate tuning or early stopping to fit.

root.chosen_best_option: ResNet-18 (pretrain prohibited, so train from scratch)

branches.option: Custom small CNN (3-4 conv blocks, ~500k parameters)

history: Custom small CNN (3-4 conv blocks, ~500k parameters)

verdict.notes: Tool choice (Jupyter + PyTorch/TF) is sound and the 30-min Colab constraint is realistic for a small CNN. The plan is well-scoped: custom architecture, ablation, and hyperparameter config are all clearly achievable. Quality is limited because the conceptual contribution is thin—training a vanilla CNN on CIFAR-10 with standard augmentation is a textbook exercise, not a systems exploration. The ablation (augmentation on/off) is straightforward but doesn't reveal deep architectural or learning dynamics. To improve: replace or *add* a second ablation comparing architectural choices (e.g., batch norm on/off, or residual blocks vs. non-residual) to deepen the insight into *why* design choices matter.

child.chosen_best_option: SGD with momentum and step-decay schedule (e.g., 0.1 initial, decay at epoch 50/80)

history: Custom small CNN (3-4 conv blocks, ~500k parameters)
Data augmentation (with vs. without)
Adam with fixed learning rate (e.g., 0.001)

verdict.notes: The plan is technically sound and will almost certainly produce a working notebook that meets the 70% accuracy floor, but it is conceptually thin for a 5-day assignment. A custom 500k-parameter CNN trained from scratch on CIFAR-10 is a standard exercise that demonstrates competent implementation rather than systems thinking or deep modeling insight. The ablation (augmentation yes/no) is legitimate but narrow. To strengthen this, consider: (1) replacing fixed learning rate with a scheduler to create a more interesting hyperparameter narrative, (2) expanding the ablation to include architectural choices (e.g., batch norm vs. dropout, or depth vs. width tradeoffs) that require genuine design reasoning, or (3) adding a second comparative axis (e.g., training dynamics under different initialization schemes). The notebook will be clear and reproducible, but the challenge level sits just above 'competent coursework' rather than 'systems-level model design.'

verdict.notes: The plan is feasible and hits all assignment hard requirements (notebook format, 70% accuracy target, ablation, config cell). Scope fit is solid—the CNN size and training time budget align well with a 5-day Colab window. Quality is limited because batch norm as the ablation is somewhat predictable; the plan would be stronger if it compared two materially different architectural choices (e.g., residual skip connections vs. none, or different augmentation strategies) that offer deeper insight into what drives performance on CIFAR-10. The fixed learning rate is also a minor weakness—a simple learning rate schedule (cosine annealing) would push accuracy closer to 75–80% and demonstrate better experimental rigor.

verdict.notes: The plan is technically sound and will almost certainly deliver a working notebook that meets the 70% accuracy bar and all stated requirements. However, the scope is somewhat mismatched: batch norm as the ablation is a reasonable but shallow choice (well-understood effect, limited insight into design tradeoffs), and the 5-day timeline for what is fundamentally a straightforward supervised learning task leaves unused capacity for deeper investigation. A stronger plan would either add a second, more conceptually interesting ablation (e.g., architecture depth vs. regularization tradeoff, or learning rate schedule sensitivity) or pivot to a systems-thinking framing (e.g., how does sample efficiency scale with model capacity, or robustness to distribution shift) to better justify the available time.

verdict.notes: Solid technical plan with appropriate tool choice (PyTorch in Colab) and a meaningful ablation that isolates a real design variable. Scope fits comfortably in 5 days. Quality is capped because the conceptual contribution is modest—data augmentation's effect on CIFAR-10 is well-established and not surprising—and the model (ResNet-18) is a standard baseline. To strengthen: consider a second ablation (e.g., batch norm vs. layer norm, or architecture depth) or a more novel comparison (e.g., mixup vs. standard augmentation) that reveals less obvious trade-offs in the domain.

branches.option: Compare two augmentation intensities (baseline: light augmentation, treatment: heavy augmentation like AutoAugment)

history: ResNet-18 (pretrain prohibited, so train from scratch)
Compare two augmentation intensities (baseline: light augmentation, treatment: heavy augmentation like AutoAugment)

verdict.notes: ResNet-18 from scratch on CIFAR-10 is a solid, well-scoped choice with a meaningful ablation (augmentation intensity). The main risk: 70% test accuracy is tight with ResNet-18 on a 30-minute budget—you'll need aggressive hyperparameter tuning or early stopping discipline to hit it reliably, and if you miss, the whole deliverable fails the spec. The ablation is good (comparing augmentation strategies is pedagogically sound), but the plan would be stronger if it also included a secondary comparison (e.g., optimizer choice or dropout rate) to hedge against missing the 70% target. Feasibility is solid (PyTorch + standard training loop = 4–5 days of work), but the tight accuracy threshold creates execution risk.

verdict.notes: ResNet-18 from scratch on CIFAR-10 is a sound, well-scoped choice with good tool fit (Colab + PyTorch). The ablation (augmentation intensity) is reasonable but somewhat shallow—it tests a hyperparameter tuning choice rather than a structural design decision. The bigger concern: 70% test accuracy from scratch in 30 minutes on a T4 is ambitious but doable; ResNet-18 typically hits ~75–78% with proper tuning. However, the plan risks spending most of the 5 days on convergence debugging and hyperparameter search rather than on conceptual depth. Consider adding a second, more fundamental ablation (e.g., batch norm on/off, or residual blocks on/off) to deepen the model understanding and reduce reliance on a single lucky run.
